seoTitle: How to Recover Unsaved Draft Emails in Outlook
metaDescription: Learn how to recover unsaved draft emails in Outlook using the Drafts folder, hidden conflict caches, crash recovery panels, and auto-save settings.
intro: A sudden crash or an accidentally closed window shouldn't mean rewriting a long email from scratch. Outlook keeps several layers of backup for unsaved drafts, from the standard Drafts folder to hidden local caches. This guide walks through each recovery path and how to shorten your auto-save interval to prevent future loss.

steps.heading: Check the Drafts folder first
steps.body: Open your Drafts folder in the left navigation panel and sort by newest to find recently closed messages.

steps.heading: Check Deleted Items
steps.body: If you accidentally discarded the draft, open Deleted Items or Trash and move the message back to your Inbox.

steps.heading: Look in Sync Issues and Conflicts
steps.body: Click the three dots at the bottom of your folder directory, select Folders view, and open Sync Issues, then Conflicts for local caches.

steps.heading: Use crash recovery on relaunch
steps.body: Reopen Outlook after a crash to trigger the automated Document Recovery pane, then click the listed item to reload your preserved text.

steps.heading: Check the web fallback
steps.body: If using Outlook Web, check your browser's cache or use the Undo option on the lower banner immediately after a mistake.

steps.heading: Shorten your auto-save interval
steps.body: Go to File, Options, Mail, scroll to Save Messages, and reduce the auto-save interval to prevent future losses.

faq.question: Where does Outlook store drafts if my computer crashes?
faq.answer: A local app data recovery folder holds temporary copies that the Document Recovery pane can restore on relaunch.

faq.question: Can I recover a draft I deleted on purpose?
faq.answer: Check the Deleted Items or Trash folder, since discarded drafts often land there before being permanently removed.

faq.question: How often does Outlook auto-save by default?
faq.answer: The default interval can be longer than ideal, so shortening it to about one minute in Mail Options reduces potential data loss.

faq.question: Does this work the same way for Outlook Web as desktop?
faq.answer: Not exactly — the web version relies more on browser cache and the Undo banner rather than a Document Recovery pane.
